sentences.text: Yeah--
I went to Konstantynow because I didn't have the guts to go back to Miedzyrzec.
I knew I have a few people from my family-- nieces and a nephew.
What I couldn't-- I make my decision to go to Konstantynow.
It was very hard to go there.
I almost lost my life there.
But I came back a few days later.
I went in under that attic, where I left them.
The nieces, with my nephew.
They all had frozen feet.
Oh, it was Esther Hofman-- no, Silverman.
Chana and Esther Goldring, Moishe [INAUDIBLE].
Rosin-- oh, my God.
Rosinger-- ?]
Only Chana is alive.
She survived on the [INAUDIBLE].
She was in Auschwitz.
She was all over.
All right.
They were crying.
They were happy to see me back, because they was outlaws.
But they opened up the ghetto two days later.
They were in the ghetto.
They were sitting all winter till Pesach.
In 1943, in a small room, very bad, no food.
But I had to go every couple days to buy, sell something, [NON-ENGLISH], go to outside of the ghetto and bring in a piece of bread or potatoes.
And that's it.
So you were helpful to supply food?
I was a father from five children.
There was nobody to supply to them.
There was no help.
What happened then?
And what was the situation in the ghetto?
In the ghetto was-- they was predicting-- that time, they was predicting [INAUDIBLE] maybe they're going to leave us.
Maybe not.
Maybe this, maybe that-- all kind of stories.
It was typhus, sickness.
It was destruction, everything, you name it.
What everybody was thinking, how can I get such a luxury [INAUDIBLE] [? death ?], like [INAUDIBLE], or the [INAUDIBLE].
I cannot-- [INAUDIBLE] I cannot die.
They called this [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
Oh, 1943, the new in ghetto Warsaw is the [NON-ENGLISH], the uprise.
In that, we were [? shaking. ?] Maybe today, maybe tomorrow, they're going to [INAUDIBLE] us.
This happened on May 2, on a Sunday morning.
This was [NON-ENGLISH]. the [NON-ENGLISH], from all the Jews from the world.
And of Saturday night, we heard [INAUDIBLE] talking between each other.
And the ghetto is surrounded with Gestapo-- all kind of tsuris.
Yiddische.
Then we start to [INAUDIBLE] ourselves.
We had a bunker in the house.
But everybody cannot fit in.
So I took my-- I left to go in my brother-in-law, my two nieces-- they went in, in the bunker.
I and my niece-- one niece and the nephew, we went on the attic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The daylight, they took us down from there.
The Gestapo and [NON-ENGLISH].
They didn't took us down by ladder.
No, they threw us down.
We get in to the unschlagplatz.
No, we went to the unschlagplatz, and we came at the platz sitting on the knees, about 2,000 people already.
I came, was maybe 1,000, but slowly, they're going to come in, They come in, they're shooting and they're knocking.
And they-- it's hard to describe.
They're beating and they're knocking, and they're doing all the kind of [? tsuris. ?]
Did they have dogs?
No.
That time-- maybe they have.
I didn't see it.
I was-- what happened?
We start to organize, me and another boy, a mishpocha, my family, a [? white ?] family, but a friend, Shmuel Goldring.
We want to send messages around.
Maybe shall we start to-- to make a riot.
The answer was from the people.
We don't want it, because I was three times jumped to the train.
I broke my arm, and I [INAUDIBLE] family.
My wife is not alive.
My children-- Who said that?
A lot of people.
You cannot blame it, because there was a run.
It was a very solid police, and the Gestapo was watching us very close.
Yeah, because [NON-ENGLISH], the uprise was in Warsaw.
And they was [? watching-- ?] they was thinking maybe this is going to be also.
What can I tell you?
About 2 o'clock, they took us to the train, five people in the row, armed.
And they walked with us.
They pushed us in, in the train.
That time, in the train, we came in.
My niece was separate.
My nephew was with me.
A cousin was with me.
The son was with me.
And we came in.
And the window was closed with metal-- not with wire.
No more wire-- a small window and a metal plate, with heavy bolts.
And we got this-- we got-- we were thinking, how can we do this?
How many people in the train?
In that train was, they expect--
It was about 60, 70 people in the wagon.
Huh?
It was the cattle cars.
Yeah.
It was prepared for more people, but a lot of them was hiding.
They didn't come out, all of them, to the-- to the transport.
I was in the train-- me, and my nephew, my cousin, my friend, and another 60, 70 people.
When we came in, we got disappointed.
It's no wire by the windows.
It's metal.
One guy was so strong, like a Superman.
He broke up the metal bolts.
And one guy, his brother said, I'm going to jump out.
I'm going to try to open the door.
He climbed out through the window, and he start to dig and dig and dig.
And he said, I can't do nothing.
Then [INAUDIBLE] told him jump.
And he jumped out.
Then some other man, from [NON-ENGLISH], he was with us.
He said, let me go.
He went out, and they start to-- and the train is going fast.
Got an express train to [INAUDIBLE] Jewish people, the people jumping out.
They want to-- what happened?
They tried.
He went.
He was digging, and digging.
And inside was a man without-- with a crutch.
He had without a leg to the knee.
Then he said to him, give me your crutch.
I want it to help me up and out.
He gave him.
He said, but give me back.
Then I thought he's going to go-- and it is to go to the crematorium.
He jumped out too, [INAUDIBLE] the train.
He came maybe 50 kilometers with that crutch.
I saw him later, a week or two weeks later, in the ghetto.
So what happened?
He opened up.
Then he said, they shoot him in the arm, They said jump.
Did you jump too?
Huh?
Wait.
I'll come to it.
So what happened?
We took inside, with [INAUDIBLE]. because he could not do anything.
[INAUDIBLE] They'd shoot him in the arm.
Then we opened up, and my friend was the third jumper, and I was the fourth.
I jumped out from the door.
A lot of them jumped out wrong.
They went in, into the train.
I jumped to the locomotive.
When I jumped out--
He was not in the train.
On the top was sitting, and the shooting, was started-- unbelievable.
Like-- I don't know what to say.
Constantly tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, tah, and so forth.
Then I jumped out.
Luckily, I was lucky.
I scratched just my hand and ear a little bit.
But then-- and I was rolling myself, and make myself dead.
What happened?
When the train left, I stand up.
I am alive.
And I start slowly to go to the woods.
Polish kids-- 20 years, 22-- they grabbed me, three of them.
They want to take me to the Gestapo.
And I said, hey, you're mistaken.
This is not the guy you're going to take in.
I have nothing to lose.
They're staying, and I'm staying.
They stay.
I cannot go away.
They're not going away, and I don't know what to do.
But I played dummy.
I stay.
I'm not going to run away.
All of a sudden, they got four more.
There's seven.
And one guy said to me, I'm not a plain man.
I am a [NON-ENGLISH].
Even one didn't say leave him alone.
So what happened?
They took me away-- my clothes, which was worthless, [NON-ENGLISH], with lice.
They took me away my watch, my few zlotys, and they let me go.
I went in, in the woods.
I was sitting.
How did you go?
Did you have any clothes left?
I got on a tee shirt.
I wearing a tee shirt.
I was freezing to death.
It was a cold night.
So what happened?
This was near the station, between Miedzyrzec and Lukov-- [NON-ENGLISH].
This was the town for the station.
And I picked up myself.
I was staying in the woods till it gets dark.
Dark, a couple people-- I hope somebody is walking at night.
I saw the people.
I recognized them.
They recognized me.
I was the leader, to go close to Miedzyrzec.
I came up a couple miles before Miedzyrzec.
We went in in the woods.
The neighbors of that-- a small town a few hours is a summer place, [?
Yelnitsa. ?] And we were sitting there.
Nobody-- nothing to eat, not to drink.
And I have not even one penny.
I said, who has money?
Give me.
I'll get bread and milk.
They gave me.
I went in.
All of a sudden, we hear--
To the houses, to the farmers.
Yeah.
I got milk and bread.
I paid him, because I didn't have money.
I got from the few people who they went with me.
In the meantime, we are shooting.
A train is going [? shooting. ?] It was another transport.
This was-- we went Sunday, and they went Monday.
On Sunday, we were sitting on the knees, Polish people, with the kids, with the corsages, they went to the church, and they was laughing of us.
This was boiling me.
I couldn't take it.
They go in with the sidewalk to the church, and laughing, the kids with the corsages.
And we're sitting on the knees going [INAUDIBLE] to be slaughtered.
In the beginning, was a lot of survivors from the jumping.
But the war took so long, they got killed, there, and there, and there, and there.
No, I left only, but after me, was a lot [? of it ?] was jumping.
To Treblinka.
I know it.
All my family went to Treblinka.
And I'll tell you a story.
When I was hesitating, shall I jump or not, but maybe I'll break a leg.
What I'm going to do?
Or maybe I'll be injured.
But in the meantime, around the 10th kilometer, I got wild.
I said, maybe let me take a chance.
What's going to be the outcome?
I became a wild man.
And I jumped out, luckily.
Then I went to my neighborhood.
And I was sitting by a Polack.
And I wanted to go see what's doing.
A friend of mine, he has a bunker by a Polish family.
I met him, and he said his brother, my friend-- he was the third jumper-- he is alive.
He came.
Then I went with him for a few days.
I was sitting in the bunker, by them.
And I went back to Miedzyrzec.
No, this was in a--
By a farmer, in a barn.
In a barn.
Huh?
Digged out and covered up with-- [NON-ENGLISH] was on top.
That's all.
Was-- I don't know.
Was digged out of a hole and put a ceiling with boards, and put up the dirt from the [NON-ENGLISH].
And this was a small hole to go in, covered from a [? mask, ?] and that's it.
Oh, at that time it was two-- started with four-- with four.
And they winded up with seven.
Two, three days.
I went back to Miedzyrzec.
Maybe anybody from my family survived.
This was May the 2, '43.
After this I was in the woods, and I had no place--
All right, I'll tell you.
I was-- I was there.
It was no place.
A big world, and for me is all things small.
I have no place to go.
I went to a farmer, and the wife was fighting with him, and I didn't want to make a bad life between the two of them account of me, and I was-- to make a long story short--
No, wait.
I went back to Miedzyrzec.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --this was July the 16th.
A couple of people from my town, they asked me, Louie, take me to [INAUDIBLE].
And I know a farm.
I will go there.
And I said yes.
In the meantime, this was-- and they was thinking-- I don't know.
Then later, I said, I'm not doing the right thing.
But I said, I'm going to keep my word.
On June 6-- July the 16th, June or July-- July the 16th, what happened?
We supposed to, at night, go out from Miedzyrzec ghetto-- about 10 people.
Around 11 o'clock, we hear how the Jewish partisans killed two-- three [NON-ENGLISH].
And then [? right ?] [? away ?] they said, are they Jews?
And we was surrounded, the whole ghetto.
Then we started to run away.
I was running, and the police was running near me, over me.
Were you running alone?
I was not.
Three people was following me.
Then I was waiting outside-- outside already, past the ghetto, past the town.
And I was waiting for the rest of the people.
They didn't come.
I was waiting and waiting and waiting and waiting.
Then I went to a farmer.
This was Friday night.
Saturday morning, people came from there.
They said to me, everyone got killed on the spot.
No more transports.
Forget it.
So what happened?
A couple hours later-- my wife today, she was that time with another girl.
She came to that farmer, and to tell my mother and the other woman, the [? landslady, ?] she [? sits ?] there, and there, and there, and there.
At night, I had to go to them.
And it was terrible to go near that village, because there they killed every Jew what they saw there.
I went there in the woods, and I got [? them ?] We came back to a farmer--
Huh?
My wife's mother, and my landslady, and another few people.
I got them out from there, and I got-- I went to that farmer.
And that farmer was one Jew.
He said, dear Louie, take the people and go.
Don't stay here, because you're going be in trouble.
No, not by this farmer.
She was a lady, a widow with kids.
She gave [INAUDIBLE]-- for a couple zlotys, she let you stay there, in a barn.
I went near Konstantynow.
On the way, we had to stop, because the daylight.
We had to sit in the field.
In the truth, I was sorry what I [? pent up ?] myself with such a-- such a problem.
Maybe they would survive.
I'm taking them probably on a [? losing ?] way.
OK, what are you going to do with them?
But I said-- I came to my neighborhood.
And I went in, in the woods, and they were sitting.
And there was a--
Near Konstantynow.
No, we took-- we was a group of 10.
You were a-- and where did you go in the woods?
In the woods, not far from Konstantynow.
And we were sitting there.
And I see it's bad, a day, two, three days, four days.
We had a Polish guy.
I have his name-- Wasla Shpura.
He was the messenger.
He was the connection guy.
He knew everybody very easily.
He was-- we paid him.
But he was good.
And he was-- he was not by himself.
So what happened?
He said, the only one way to do-- go in that section.
I'll take you there.
And go at night.
In the morning, I'll come out, And I'm going to call the dog's name.
Then you come out.
And there is a cemetery-- prisoners, Russian prisoners, was killed there-- about six, seven.
The bones is all around.
There were Jews-- the Polish people were afraid to go there.
Then you got to make a bunker and stay there.
This is what we did.
We took out the people from the woods, go back in the other forest, and we made--
The cemetery was from bones, was [INAUDIBLE] skeletons, from the people.
We made it.
We made it-- me and a few of them.
We did this.
And there we were sitting.
But we was not enough.
We made maybe 10 more bunkers.
If this was [? built ?] up, then we go to the other one.
And this was-- we're sitting from June 16 or July 16 till July the 30th, 1944.
Not in the same area.
It was the wider sections-- other sections.
Around Konstantynow.
The woods-- the way-- the woods neighborhood was called [?
Walima-- ?] [?
Walima. ?]
We got a few-- [INAUDIBLE], but he was baking bread in night time, we should pick up.
We paid him.
And potatoes, we were stealing.
Water we had to steal.
We had to go far, far for water.
We were about 60 people-- and 50.
And six people got killed nine days before the liberation.
One man--
[INAUDIBLE] got killed-- the [?
AK ?] was in the woods.
The [NON-ENGLISH].
The code word.
[NON-ENGLISH] .
And they caught one guy there, and they took him to the other place, to show where the other one, and they killed him-- six people.
And one guy, he was twice in Treblinka.
He struggled to go out into the [NON-ENGLISH].
And he escaped.
And these Polish people killed him.
We was divided.
We were separate-- five in one place, five in the other place, because we didn't want to put in the [? cheese ?] in one cake.
Yes, yes, was another guy.
He was trying to help out.
What busy?
We was laying like dead people, and that's it.
Inside it was looking-- a very small place for six people.
And the mattress was from branches from the birch leaves.
And if you have to go, for example, to make a pee pee, you had to wake up the other one.
You couldn't go out.
We was-- [CLAPS].
Very close.
It was a-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Then we was-- we came to our city, Konstantynow.
After the war.
The Russian liberates us, the Russians, in July the 30th.
I was [INAUDIBLE] for a couple days by a Polish family.
But the other ones in--
We know the Germans are going to break their necks.
They're going to lose.
But we-- but we are going to survive.
This what was our talking.
We had connections with the news, from the radio, from the Polish peoples, because I picked up [? one ?] of [? five ?] from that, when I went there.
Oh, I was in that time in a Polish family, also in a bunker.
But when I heard, I looked out a little hole.
I saw the Russian people.
And the housewife, the Polish, she told us already.
They're coming in.
They're already not far away.
When the Russians come?
We left the woods, the people.
We went into the city.
It was very, very hard to step into that town-- very hard.
You see your house, your brother's house, with the children, with your family, and to see the people, which they belong there, and they're laughing of you.
Yeah.
The Polish people.
They was wondering, where were you?
We was looking for you.
I know you was looking-- to kill.
One fellow told me, a Polish fellow, Louie, watch yourself, [? for ?] they're all your friends, because they want to kill you even now.
And it's not Germans they're going to kill you.
They killed a lot of Jews after the war, when the Russians-- when the Germans was not there anymore.
All right, good.
Go ahead.
